title: DHS Is Installing Hundreds of AI Towers That Decide Who Looks Suspicious
narrative: Customs and Border Protection says its new Autonomous Surveillance Towers make the border safer and more efficient. The AI-powered systems detect, identify, and track movement without requiring a human to watch in real time. Commissioner Rodney Scott says the technology is ahead of schedule and under budget. Congress allocated $2.7 billion for the consolidated tower program. CBP is ahead of schedule on both wall construction and technology rollout. The public framing: smarter borders, fewer crossings, better outcomes for agents and communities alike.
telemetry: ANDURIL-363M: $362,974,500 SBIR Phase III IDIQ contract awarded December 2025 — largest single autonomous surveillance tower award in DHS history
AST-DEPLOY: 300+ Anduril towers already operational by late 2024, covering approximately 30% of US land border
CBP-SCALE: 148 existing towers getting AI upgrades in 2026; 50 new towers planned; target is 890 total autonomous towers
GDIT-1.8B: GDIT positioned on $1.8 billion IDIQ contract vehicle for integrated surveillance — competing for next-generation contracts
M2S2-RANGE: Modular Mobile Surveillance System trucks detect movement several miles out; location accuracy within 250 feet, stretch goal 50 feet
M2S2-AUTO: M2S2 operates in dual mode — agent-present or fully unattended; onboard AI sends alerts to remote operators without human in loop
RAND-27PCT: RAND study (RR-4348, 2020) — Integrated Fixed Towers produced ~27% fewer apprehensions in treated zones; migrants diverted, not apprehended
WATER-DOUBLE: 2025 Political Geography study — crossers avoiding surveillance zones suffer double the water loss; rerouting through more arid, dangerous terrain
PERSONNEL+: CBP 2022 document: autonomous enhancements reduce personnel required to monitor surveillance systems
TARS-AUTO: Tethered Aerostat Radar System — automates EO/IR sensor operations with AI image analysis, no human in loop for detection decisions
analysis:
The official narrative frames autonomous surveillance towers as a force multiplier — fewer agents needed to watch more border. The technology is presented as benign, even inevitable: AI that detects, alerts, and protects. But the telemetry tells a different story.

Anduril has already deployed over 300 towers under a $363 million contract. GDIT is competing for the next tranche. The M2S2 trucks can be parked in terrain no fixed tower can reach, operate all night without a human watching the feed, and feed targeting data to other DHS assets including drones. The system doesn't just watch — it decides what's worth alerting on. That's the agentic layer. A machine looks at a thermal signature or a movement pattern and decides, without a human in the loop, that this particular person crossing at 3am through a remote sector warrants a dispatch.

The academic record adds a brutal counterpoint. RAND's 2020 study found towers produced roughly 27% fewer apprehensions in their zones — not because agents got better at catching people, but because crossings were diverted elsewhere. The machines pushed traffic to more dangerous routes. The 2025 Political Geography study confirmed the bodily cost: crossers rerouted around surveillance zones lost twice as much water, adding measurable mortality risk to an already lethal crossing environment.

The policy gap is the real story. The technology is operational. The funding is locked in. And there is no published DHS framework governing when an AI-decided alert escalates to a drone dispatch, a vehicle pursuit, or an agent response. CBP says autonomous enhancements reduce personnel required to monitor surveillance. That also means fewer humans exercising judgment at the moment of decision. The towers are in the field. The machine is making calls. Nobody wrote the rules.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT] The autonomous surveillance apparatus is already operational — it decides, tracks, and dispatches without a human in the loop, and the policy framework to govern it doesn't exist.
